ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean economic rice (cai fan). FOODON_00002678 (cooked rice-based dish) is the closest defensible parent class. Exact term unavailable as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Singaporean cai fan / economic rice. A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Exact code unavailable as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to economic rice / cai fan as a specific food entity.

main_ingredients: fried meat (variable — pork, chicken, or fish)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of economic rice with one vegetable dish and two fried meat dishes typically weighs approximately 350 g as served at a Singapore hawker centre or coffee shop.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (1 Vegetable, 2 Fried Meats) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Rice (1 Vegetable, 2 Fried Meats), CR ID F05100033. All per-100g values are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is set to -1. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (steamed, Asian-style) analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean economic rice (cai fan) as a composite dish. GI of 72 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for steamed white rice (Asian-style), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 63.7) / 100 ≈ 45.8, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 18.19 × 3.5 = 63.7 g. The fried meat and vegetable components are not expected to materially alter the GI of the rice base.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 311 mg/100 g with low dietary fibre (0.91 g/100 g) and absent potassium data; fried meats contribute saturated fat promoting arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request less sauce, and substitute fried meats with steamed or braised options to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,089 mg sodium, which is 47% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI 72) with 18.19 g/100 g carbohydrate and only 0.91 g/100 g dietary fibre; fried meats add caloric density and saturated fat that worsen insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce the rice portion, increase the vegetable component, and replace fried meats with steamed or braised protein to lower the glycaemic load and improve postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fat at 9.16 g/100 g predominantly from deep-frying; high sodium (311 mg/100 g); fried preparation generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and oxidised lipids that promote atherosclerosis
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid fried meat selections and choose steamed or braised alternatives, request reduced sauce, and limit portion size to manage sodium, saturated fat, and AGE exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried meat components generate AGEs that directly worsen joint inflammation; high fat content from frying is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace fried meat selections with steamed fish or braised tofu and increase vegetable portions to boost antioxidant intake and reduce AGE-driven joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic rice, known locally as cai fan (菜饭) or nasi ekonomi, is one of Singapore's most ubiquitous hawker and coffee-shop meals. This variant consists of steamed white rice served with one stir-fried or blanched vegetable dish and two deep-fried meat dishes — typically items such as fried pork chop, fried chicken, fried fish, or fried tofu. The dish is assembled cafeteria-style at hawker stalls where diners select their preferred accompaniments from a display of pre-cooked dishes. The rice is soft and slightly sticky; the fried meats are crispy on the outside with a savoury, oily flavour profile; the vegetable dish adds colour and mild bitterness or sweetness depending on the selection. Per 100 g, the dish provides 188 kcal, 7.65 g protein, 9.16 g fat, 18.19 g carbohydrate, 0.91 g dietary fibre, and 311 mg sodium. The high sodium content (311 mg/100 g) and deep-fried preparation make this dish a moderate-to-high concern for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index (GI 72), and a standard 350 g plate delivers approximately 63.7 g carbohydrate and a glycaemic load of approximately 45.8. This dish is a staple working-lunch option across Singapore's multiethnic population and is widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and coffee shops throughout the island.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cai fan?
Economic rice with fried meat nutrition
Is cai fan suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05100033) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate analogue, which is a well-established and defensible approximation for this dish type.
notes: Fields set to -1 due to data unavailability in the HPB source: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of the access date. Detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this composite hawker dish. The dietary_tags in the input ('vegetarian', 'plant_based') have been removed as this dish contains fried meats and is not vegetarian or plant-based. Halal status is not confirmed as fried meat selections vary by stall and may include pork. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (Asian-style) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources.
